title: 74.	Find number of subarrays with gcd = 1 (gcd stack trick).
description: 74.	Find number of subarrays with gcd = 1 (gcd stack trick).

examples.explanation: Array: [1, 2, 3], n=3. Initialize count=0. List all subarrays: (0,0): [1], GCD(1)=1, count=1. (0,1): [1,2], compute GCD: start with 1, GCD(1,2)=1, count=2. (0,2): [1,2,3], GCD: 1 with 2 gives 1, then 1 with 3 gives 1, count=3. (1,1): [2], GCD(2)=2, not 1. (1,2): [2,3], GCD(2,3)=1, count=4. (2,2): [3], GCD(3)=3, not 1. Total subarrays: 6, count=4? Wait, check: All subarrays: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3], [2], [2,3], [3]. GCDs: 1,1,1,2,1,3. Count where GCD=1: 4. But output should be 4, not 6. Correcting: For arr=[1,2,3], subarrays with GCD=1: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3], [2,3]. That's 4. So output=4. Trace: Start i=0: j=0: subarray [1], GCD=1, count=1. j=1: [1,2], GCD(1,2)=1, count=2. j=2: [1,2,3], GCD: gcd_val=1, update with 2 gives 1, with 3 gives 1, count=3. i=1: j=1: [2], GCD=2, not 1. j=2: [2,3], GCD(2,3)=1, count=4. i=2: j=2: [3], GCD=3, not 1. Final count=4.

Array: [2, 4, 6], n=3. Initialize count=0. i=0: j=0: [2], GCD=2. j=1: [2,4], GCD(2,4)=2. j=2: [2,4,6], GCD: start 2, with 4 gives 2, with 6 gives 2. i=1: j=1: [4], GCD=4. j=2: [4,6], GCD(4,6)=2. i=2: j=2: [6], GCD=6. All GCDs are >1, so count=0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Check all possible subarrays by iterating through all start and end indices, computing GCD for each subarray. This is the most straightforward but inefficient approach that serves as a baseline.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with GCD = 1 Using Brute Force O(n³)
ladders.desc: Complete solution that counts all subarrays where the GCD of all elements equals 1 by checking every possible subarray.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the problem. The intuition is straightforward: to find all subarrays with GCD = 1, we must examine every possible contiguous subarray in the array. Since we need to verify the GCD condition for each subarray, we generate all start and end indices, compute the GCD for each subarray, and count those where GCD equals 1. The algorithm works by using three nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index, the middle loop fixes the ending index, and the inner loop computes the GCD of elements from start to end. We initialize a counter to zero. For each start index i from 0 to n-1, for each end index j from i to n-1, we compute the GCD of elements from index i to j. To compute GCD, we initialize gcd_val to the first element (arr[i]) and iterate through elements from i+1 to j, updating gcd_val using the Euclidean algorithm (gcd_val = gcd(gcd_val, arr[k])). If at any point gcd_val becomes 1, we can break early since GCD won't change (GCD of any set with 1 is 1), but in worst case, we compute fully. After computing, if gcd_val == 1, we increment the counter. Finally, return the counter. Time complexity is O(n³) because we have O(n²) subarrays and computing GCD for each takes O(n) in worst case (though GCD computation is O(log min(a,b)) per pair, but iterating through elements gives O(n) per subarray). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach serves as a baseline for optimization but is inefficient for large n. Connection to higher levels: This is the full implementation using the brute force method. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays (should return 0), incorrect GCD computation (e.g., using product instead), or off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: Use nested loops and a helper function for GCD.

examples.trace: Array: [1, 2, 3], n=3. Initialize count=0. List all subarrays: (0,0): [1], GCD(1)=1, count=1. (0,1): [1,2], compute GCD: start with 1, GCD(1,2)=1, count=2. (0,2): [1,2,3], GCD: 1 with 2 gives 1, then 1 with 3 gives 1, count=3. (1,1): [2], GCD(2)=2, not 1. (1,2): [2,3], GCD(2,3)=1, count=4. (2,2): [3], GCD(3)=3, not 1. Total subarrays: 6, count=4? Wait, check: All subarrays: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3], [2], [2,3], [3]. GCDs: 1,1,1,2,1,3. Count where GCD=1: 4. But output should be 4, not 6. Correcting: For arr=[1,2,3], subarrays with GCD=1: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3], [2,3]. That's 4. So output=4. Trace: Start i=0: j=0: subarray [1], GCD=1, count=1. j=1: [1,2], GCD(1,2)=1, count=2. j=2: [1,2,3], GCD: gcd_val=1, update with 2 gives 1, with 3 gives 1, count=3. i=1: j=1: [2], GCD=2, not 1. j=2: [2,3], GCD(2,3)=1, count=4. i=2: j=2: [3], GCD=3, not 1. Final count=4.

Array: [2, 4, 6], n=3. Initialize count=0. i=0: j=0: [2], GCD=2. j=1: [2,4], GCD(2,4)=2. j=2: [2,4,6], GCD: start 2, with 4 gives 2, with 6 gives 2. i=1: j=1: [4], GCD=4. j=2: [4,6], GCD(4,6)=2. i=2: j=2: [6], GCD=6. All GCDs are >1, so count=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute GCD for a Subarray Using Iterative Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Helper function that calculates the GCD of all elements in a given subarray from index start to end.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core helper function used in the brute force approach to compute the GCD of a contiguous subarray. The intuition is that the GCD of multiple numbers can be computed iteratively by taking the GCD of the first two numbers, then the result with the next number, and so on, using the property that GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(GCD(a,b), c). This relies on the Euclidean algorithm, which states that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). The algorithm: Given an array arr and indices start and end (inclusive), if start > end, return 0 (or handle error). Initialize gcd_val to arr[start]. For each index k from start+1 to end, update gcd_val = gcd(gcd_val, arr[k]) using the Euclidean algorithm. The Euclidean algorithm can be implemented iteratively: while b != 0: a, b = b, a % b, starting with a = gcd_val, b = arr[k]. After the loop, a is the GCD. Alternatively, use math.gcd in Python. If at any point gcd_val becomes 1, we can break early since GCD with 1 will remain 1. Return gcd_val. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O((end-start+1) * log(min(a,b))) in worst case, but simplified as O(n) for the subarray length. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to level 0: This function is called for each subarray in the nested loops. Common mistakes include not handling empty subarrays, incorrect update order, or infinite loops in Euclidean algorithm if not checking for zero. Real code pattern: Loop through indices and update GCD iteratively.

examples.trace: Subarray: [6, 10, 15]. Initialize gcd_val = arr[0] = 6. k=1: arr[1]=10. Compute GCD(6,10): a=6, b=10. Iteration 1: b=10 !=0, a,b = 10, 6%10=6 → a=10, b=6. Iteration 2: b=6 !=0, a,b = 6, 10%6=4 → a=6, b=4. Iteration 3: b=4 !=0, a,b = 4, 6%4=2 → a=4, b=2. Iteration 4: b=2 !=0, a,b = 2, 4%2=0 → a=2, b=0. Loop ends, GCD=2. Update gcd_val=2. k=2: arr[2]=15. Compute GCD(2,15): a=2, b=15. Iteration 1: b=15 !=0, a,b = 15, 2%15=2 → a=15, b=2. Iteration 2: b=2 !=0, a,b = 2, 15%2=1 → a=2, b=1. Iteration 3: b=1 !=0, a,b = 1, 2%1=0 → a=1, b=0. Loop ends, GCD=1. Update gcd_val=1. Return 1.

Subarray: [8, 12]. Initialize gcd_val = arr[1] = 8. k=2: arr[2]=12. Compute GCD(8,12): a=8, b=12. Iteration 1: b=12 !=0, a,b = 12, 8%12=8 → a=12, b=8. Iteration 2: b=8 !=0, a,b = 8, 12%8=4 → a=8, b=4. Iteration 3: b=4 !=0, a,b = 4, 8%4=0 → a=4, b=0. Loop ends, GCD=4. Update gcd_val=4. Return 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Subarrays Using Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays by varying start and end indices with two nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the central logic of the brute force approach: generating every possible subarray to check. The intuition is that to examine all contiguous subarrays, we need to consider every starting index and every ending index that is at least the starting index. A subarray is defined by two indices, start (i) and end (j), where i ≤ j. By iterating i from 0 to n-1 and for each i, iterating j from i to n-1, we cover all possible subarrays. The total number of subarrays is n*(n+1)/2, which is O(n²). The algorithm: Initialize an empty list or counter. For i in range(0, n): For j in range(i, n): The subarray is from index i to j inclusive. We can process it directly (e.g., compute GCD) or store indices. This ensures we don't miss any subarray and don't count any twice. Time complexity is O(n²) for generating subarrays, but when combined with GCD computation, it becomes O(n³). Space complexity is O(1) if processing on the fly. Connection to level 0: This loop structure is used in the full solution to iterate over subarrays. Common mistakes include using j from 0 to n-1 (which includes non-contiguous or reversed subarrays), or off-by-one errors (e.g., using j < n instead of j <= n-1). Real code pattern: Two nested for-loops with i and j.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understand GCD Properties for Multiple Numbers
ladders.desc: Learn how GCD extends from two numbers to multiple numbers and its iterative computation.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for computing GCD of subarrays. The intuition is that the Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) of a set of numbers is the largest positive integer that divides all numbers in the set. For two numbers, GCD can be computed using the Euclidean algorithm. For multiple numbers, the GCD can be found iteratively: GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(GCD(a,b), c). This property holds because GCD is associative and commutative. The algorithm to compute GCD of a list: Start with result = first number. For each subsequent number in the list, update result = GCD(result, current number). Continue until all numbers are processed. If at any point result becomes 1, you can stop early since GCD with 1 is always 1. This is efficient because GCD computation for two numbers is fast (O(log min(a,b))). Time complexity for k numbers is O(k * log(min(a,b))). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to level 1: This property is used in the sub-routine to compute GCD for subarrays. Common mistakes include assuming GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(a, GCD(b,c)) which is correct but order doesn't matter, or trying to compute GCD by multiplying numbers. Real code pattern: Iterate through array and update GCD using math.gcd or custom function.

examples.trace: Compute GCD iteratively: Start with result=12. Next number: 18. GCD(12,18): Euclidean algorithm: 18 % 12 = 6, 12 % 6 = 0, so GCD=6. Update result=6. Next number: 24. GCD(6,24): 24 % 6 = 0, so GCD=6. Update result=6. Final GCD=6.
Start result=7. Next: 14. GCD(7,14): 14 % 7 = 0, GCD=7. Update result=7. Next: 21. GCD(7,21): 21 % 7 = 0, GCD=7. Update result=7. Final GCD=7.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute GCD of Two Numbers Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the Euclidean algorithm to find the greatest common divisor of two integers.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in the brute force approach. The intuition behind the Euclidean algorithm is based on the property that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). By repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of the division, we reduce the numbers until the remainder is zero, at which point the other number is the GCD. The algorithm: Given two integers a and b (non-negative), if b is 0, return a. Otherwise, while b != 0: set a, b = b, a % b. After the loop, a is the GCD. This works because each step reduces the numbers, and GCD remains invariant. Time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) as the numbers decrease quickly. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to level 3: This operation is the building block for computing GCD of multiple numbers. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops if not updating correctly, or assuming a > b (algorithm works regardless). Real code pattern: Use a while loop or recursion.

examples.trace: Start: a=48, b=18. Since b!=0: Compute a % b = 48 % 18 = 12. Update: a=18, b=12. b!=0: 18 % 12 = 6. Update: a=12, b=6. b!=0: 12 % 6 = 0. Update: a=6, b=0. b=0, loop ends. GCD=6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Subarrays and Contiguous Sequences
ladders.desc: Learn what a subarray is and how to identify all contiguous subsequences of an array.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the problem. A subarray is a contiguous sequence of elements within an array. For example, in array [1,2,3], the subarrays are [1], [2], [3], [1,2], [2,3], and [1,2,3]. Contiguous means the elements are consecutive in the original array, without gaps. To find all subarrays, you need to consider every possible starting index and every possible ending index that is at or after the starting index. The total number of subarrays in an array of length n is n*(n+1)/2, which comes from summing the number of subarrays ending at each position. The intuition: For each starting point i, you can end at any j from i to n-1, giving (n-i) choices. Summing over i gives the total. This concept is foundational because the problem requires examining all such subarrays. Time complexity to list all subarrays is O(n²) if you generate them, but just counting is O(1). Space complexity depends on storage. Connection to level 2: This understanding is used to iterate over subarrays. Common mistakes include confusing subarrays with subsequences (which can be non-contiguous) or subsets. Real code pattern: Think in terms of start and end indices.

examples.trace: Array length n=3. Start i=0: end j=0 → [4], j=1 → [4,5], j=2 → [4,5,6]. i=1: j=1 → [5], j=2 → [5,6]. i=2: j=2 → [6]. Total 6 subarrays.

approaches.name: GCD Stack Trick O(n log M)
approaches.summary: Maintain a stack of (gcd_value, count) pairs while iterating through the array. For each new element, update the stack by merging with previous GCD values and counting subarrays ending at current index with GCD=1. This efficiently handles the fact that GCD values change slowly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with GCD = 1 Using Stack Trick
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all subarrays where GCD equals 1 using the GCD stack optimization.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires counting all contiguous subarrays where the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all elements equals 1. A naive approach would check all O(n²) subarrays, each taking O(n) time for GCD computation, resulting in O(n³) complexity. The GCD stack trick optimizes this to O(n log M) by leveraging two key insights: 1) For a fixed ending index, GCD values of subarrays ending there form a non-increasing sequence with limited distinct values (at most log M where M is max element). 2) We can maintain these GCD values in a stack while iterating through the array.

Intuition: When we extend a subarray by adding a new element, the GCD either stays the same or decreases (since GCD(a,b) ≤ min(a,b)). As we move the ending index rightward, we need to update GCDs for all subarrays ending at previous positions. Instead of recomputing from scratch, we maintain a stack of (gcd_value, count) pairs where count represents how many subarrays ending at current position have that GCD value. For each new element, we compute GCD with previous stack values and merge duplicates.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize answer = 0, stack = []
2. For each element x in array:
   a. Create new stack new_stack = [(x, 1)]
   b. For each (g, cnt) in current stack:
      - new_g = gcd(g, x)
      - If new_g equals last GCD in new_stack, merge counts
      - Else append (new_g, cnt) to new_stack
   c. Replace stack with new_stack
   d. Add to answer the count of pairs where GCD = 1
3. Return answer

Time Complexity: O(n log M) where M is max element. Each element processes at most log M distinct GCD values in stack. Space: O(log M) for stack.

Connection to L1: This uses the stack update subroutine from L1 as its core operation.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to merge consecutive equal GCDs (wasting space), incorrect counting logic (should count all subarrays ending at current index with GCD=1), off-by-one errors in index handling.

Real code pattern:
def count_subarrays_gcd_one(arr):
    ans = 0
    stack = []  # list of (gcd, count)
    for x in arr:
        new_stack = [(x, 1)]
        for g, cnt in stack:
            new_g = math.gcd(g, x)
            if new_stack[-1][0] == new_g:
                new_stack[-1] = (new_g, new_stack[-1][1] + cnt)
            else:
                new_stack.append((new_g, cnt))
        stack = new_stack
        for g, cnt in stack:
            if g == 1:
                ans += cnt
    return ans

examples.trace:
Array: [2,4,6,3]
Index 0 (x=2): stack=[(2,1)], GCD=1 count=0, ans=0
Index 1 (x=4):
  Process stack [(2,1)]: gcd(2,4)=2
  new_stack starts as [(4,1)], then add (2,1) → [(4,1),(2,1)]
  stack=[(4,1),(2,1)], GCD=1 count=0, ans=0
Index 2 (x=6):
  Process stack [(4,1),(2,1)]:
    gcd(4,6)=2, new_stack=[(6,1)], merge with (2,1)? No, different → [(6,1),(2,1)]
    gcd(2,6)=2, last in new_stack is 2, merge: [(6,1),(2,2)]
  stack=[(6,1),(2,2)], GCD=1 count=0, ans=0
Index 3 (x=3):
  Process stack [(6,1),(2,2)]:
    gcd(6,3)=3, new_stack=[(3,1)]
    gcd(2,3)=1, different from 3 → [(3,1),(1,2)]
  stack=[(3,1),(1,2)], GCD=1 count=2, ans=2
  Subarrays with GCD=1 ending at index 3: [3], [6,3], [2,6,3] (but wait, need to verify)
  Actually: For stack (3,1): subarray [3] has GCD=3≠1
          For stack (1,2): subarrays [4,6,3] and [2,4,6,3] have GCD=1
  Total subarrays with GCD=1 in entire array: [3], [6,3], [4,6,3], [2,4,6,3] = 4
Final answer: 4

Array: [1,2,3]
Index 0 (x=1): stack=[(1,1)], GCD=1 count=1, ans=1
Index 1 (x=2):
  Process stack [(1,1)]: gcd(1,2)=1
  new_stack starts as [(2,1)], then process (1,1)→gcd=1, merge with last? last is 2≠1 → [(2,1),(1,1)]
  stack=[(2,1),(1,1)], GCD=1 count=1, ans=2
Index 2 (x=3):
  Process stack [(2,1),(1,1)]:
    gcd(2,3)=1, new_stack=[(3,1)], then (1,1)→gcd=1, merge: [(3,1),(1,2)]
  stack=[(3,1),(1,2)], GCD=1 count=2, ans=4
  Subarrays with GCD=1: [1], [1,2], [2], [1,2,3], [2,3], [3] = 6
Final answer: 6

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update GCD Stack for New Element
ladders.desc: Maintain and update stack of (gcd, count) pairs when processing a new array element.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the heart of the GCD stack trick. Given a current stack representing GCD values for subarrays ending at previous index, and a new element x, we need to compute the new stack for subarrays ending at current index.

Intuition: For subarrays ending at previous index i-1, we have various GCD values g with counts c (meaning c subarrays ending at i-1 have GCD g). When we extend these subarrays to include element at index i, their GCD becomes gcd(g, x). Since GCD is associative, we don't need to recompute from scratch. The key observation is that gcd(g, x) values will be non-increasing as we process the stack (since original g values are non-increasing). This allows efficient merging of consecutive equal GCD values.

Algorithm:
1. Start with new_stack containing (x, 1) representing the single-element subarray [x]
2. For each pair (g, cnt) in current stack (processed in order):
   a. Compute new_g = gcd(g, x)
   b. If new_g equals the GCD value at top of new_stack:
      - Merge by adding cnt to the count of top element
   c. Otherwise:
      - Append (new_g, cnt) to new_stack
3. Return new_stack

Time Complexity: O(k) where k is stack size (≤ log M). Space: O(log M).

Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each array element in the full solution.

Connection to L2: This implements the core insight that GCD values change slowly.

Common mistakes: Processing stack in wrong order (must maintain non-increasing GCD order), not merging equal GCDs (leads to O(n²) worst case), incorrect initialization of new_stack.

Real code pattern:
def update_gcd_stack(stack, x):
    new_stack = [(x, 1)]
    for g, cnt in stack:
        new_g = math.gcd(g, x)
        if new_stack[-1][0] == new_g:
            new_stack[-1] = (new_g, new_stack[-1][1] + cnt)
        else:
            new_stack.append((new_g, cnt))
    return new_stack

examples.trace:
Initial new_stack = [(3,1)]
Process stack element (6,1): gcd(6,3)=3
  new_stack top is (3,1), same GCD 3 → merge: new_stack = [(3,2)]
Process stack element (2,2): gcd(2,3)=1
  new_stack top is (3,2), different GCD 1 → append: new_stack = [(3,2), (1,2)]
But wait, careful: Actually first element should be just (3,1) for subarray [3], then merging happens with (6,1) which gives another (3,1) but that's for subarray [6,3]. Let's trace properly:
Start: new_stack = [(3,1)] for subarray [3]
Process (6,1): gcd(6,3)=3, same as top 3 → merge: (3,1+1)=(3,2)
  Now new_stack represents: (3,2) for subarrays [3] and [6,3]
Process (2,2): gcd(2,3)=1, different from 3 → append (1,2)
  Final: [(3,2), (1,2)] for subarrays: [3],[6,3] have GCD=3; [2,6,3],[4,6,3] have GCD=1
Output: [(3,2), (1,2)]

Initial new_stack = [(6,1)]
Process (4,1): gcd(4,6)=2
  new_stack top is (6,1), different GCD 2 → append: [(6,1), (2,1)]
Process (2,1): gcd(2,6)=2
  new_stack top is (2,1), same GCD 2 → merge: [(6,1), (2,2)]
Output: [(6,1), (2,2)]

ladders.title: Core Logic — GCD Values Change Slowly (Limited Distinct Values)
ladders.desc: Understanding why subarray GCDs have at most O(log M) distinct values for fixed ending index.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental insight enabling the stack optimization. For all subarrays ending at a particular index i, the GCD values form a non-increasing sequence with limited distinct values—specifically at most log₂(M) + 1 where M is the maximum array element.

Intuition: Consider all subarrays ending at position i: [i], [i-1,i], [i-2,i], ..., [0,i]. Their GCD values g₀, g₁, g₂, ... have two properties:
1. Non-increasing: g₀ ≥ g₁ ≥ g₂ ≥ ... because adding more elements to the left can only decrease or maintain GCD (GCD(a,b) ≤ min(a,b)).
2. Each GCD divides the previous one: gⱼ divides gⱼ₋₁. This is because gⱼ = GCD(prefix up to j, aᵢ) and gⱼ₋₁ = GCD(prefix up to j-1, aᵢ), and GCD of more numbers divides GCD of fewer numbers.

From these properties, when GCD decreases, it must decrease by at least a factor of 2 (except when becoming 1). Reason: If gⱼ < gⱼ₋₁, then gⱼ ≤ gⱼ₋₁/2 because gⱼ divides gⱼ₋₁ and is strictly smaller. The worst case is when gⱼ = gⱼ₋₁/2. Starting from maximum value M, you can halve at most log₂(M) times before reaching 1.

Thus, for any fixed ending index, there are at most log₂(M) + 1 distinct GCD values. This bound makes maintaining a stack efficient—stack size is O(log M) rather than O(n).

Connection to L1: This explains why the stack update subroutine has O(log M) complexity per element.

Connection to L3: This builds on the mathematical property that GCD is non-increasing when extending subarrays.

Common mistakes: Confusing this with total subarrays (O(n²)), not understanding why the halving argument works, missing the 'divides' relationship.

Real code pattern: While this is a conceptual insight rather than code, it justifies:
max_distinct_gcds = int(math.log2(max(arr))) + 2

examples.trace:
Subarrays ending at index 3 (element 24):
[24] → GCD=24
[16,24] → GCD(16,24)=8
[12,16,24] → GCD(12,8)=4 (since GCD(12,16,24)=GCD(12,GCD(16,24))=GCD(12,8)=4)
[8,12,16,24] → GCD(8,4)=4
Sequence: 24, 8, 4, 4 (non-increasing)
Distinct values: 24, 8, 4 (3 values)
Max element M=24, log₂(24)≈4.6, bound is 5+1=6, actual 3 ≤ bound.
Notice 24→8 (factor 3), 8→4 (factor 2).

Subarrays ending at index 2 (element 15):
[15] → GCD=15
[10,15] → GCD(10,15)=5
[6,10,15] → GCD(6,5)=1
Sequence: 15, 5, 1 (non-increasing)
Distinct values: 15, 5, 1 (3 values)
Max element M=15, log₂(15)≈3.9, bound is 4+1=5, actual 3 ≤ bound.
Notice 15→5 (factor 3), 5→1 (factor 5). The halving argument gives upper bound, not exact halving each time.

ladders.title: Building Block — GCD Properties for Subarrays
ladders.desc: How GCD behaves when extending subarrays and combining GCD computations.
ladders.explanation:
This level covers essential mathematical properties of GCD that make the stack trick possible. Understanding these properties is crucial for designing the algorithm.

Key Properties:
1. GCD is associative and commutative: GCD(a,b,c) = GCD(GCD(a,b),c) = GCD(a,GCD(b,c)). This allows incremental computation.
2. Non-increasing property: When extending a subarray to include a new element on the right, the GCD either stays the same or decreases. Formally, for any subarray A[i:j] and element x, GCD(A[i:j] + [x]) ≤ GCD(A[i:j]). This happens because GCD(a,b) ≤ min(a,b).
3. Divisor chain property: If you have consecutive subarrays ending at same position, their GCD values form a sequence where each divides the previous. Specifically, for subarrays ending at index k: let g₁ = GCD(A[i:k]), g₂ = GCD(A[i-1:k]), then g₂ divides g₁. This follows from GCD of more numbers dividing GCD of fewer numbers.
4. GCD computation is fast: Using Euclidean algorithm, gcd(a,b) takes O(log min(a,b)) time.

Intuition for algorithm design: Property 1 allows us to compute GCD of extended subarray as gcd(previous_gcd, new_element). Property 2 ensures GCD values in our stack are non-increasing. Property 3 gives the halving argument for limited distinct values. Property 4 makes each GCD computation efficient.

Connection to L2: These properties justify why GCD values change slowly.

Connection to L4: This builds on basic GCD computation from L4.

Common mistakes: Assuming GCD always decreases (it can stay same), not understanding associativity for multiple elements, confusing GCD with LCM properties.

Real code pattern: These properties inform the algorithm structure:
# Property 1 in action:
new_gcd = math.gcd(prev_gcd, new_element)
# Property 2 ensures stack GCDs are sorted
assert all(stack[i][0] >= stack[i+1][0] for i in range(len(stack)-1))

examples.trace:
Using associativity: GCD(6,10,15) = GCD(GCD(6,10), 15) = GCD(2, 15) = 1
Notice GCD decreased from 2 to 1.
Check non-increasing property: 2 ≥ 1 ✓
Check divisor property: 1 divides 2 ✓
Direct computation: Factors of 6:1,2,3,6; 10:1,2,5,10; 15:1,3,5,15. Common factor: 1.

Using associativity: GCD(12,18,24) = GCD(GCD(12,18), 24) = GCD(6, 24) = 6
GCD stayed same at 6.
Check non-increasing property: 6 ≥ 6 ✓ (equal is allowed)
Check divisor property: 6 divides 6 ✓
Direct computation: Factors of 12:1,2,3,4,6,12; 18:1,2,3,6,9,18; 24:1,2,3,4,6,8,12,24. Common factors: 1,2,3,6. Max is 6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute GCD of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Efficiently compute greatest common divisor using Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation:
The GCD (Greatest Common Divisor) of two integers is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers without remainder. For the stack trick, we need to compute GCD repeatedly and efficiently.

Euclidean Algorithm: Based on the property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). We repeatedly replace larger number with remainder until remainder is 0. The last non-zero remainder is the GCD.

Intuition: If d divides both a and b, then d also divides a - b, and more generally a mod b (since a mod b = a - q*b). By reducing the problem size each step, we quickly reach the GCD.

Algorithm (iterative):
1. While b ≠ 0:
   a, b = b, a % b
2. Return a (absolute value)

Time Complexity: O(log min(a,b)) because each step reduces numbers significantly (at least halving every two steps). Space: O(1).

Example: gcd(48, 18)
48 ÷ 18 = 2 remainder 12 → gcd(18, 12)
18 ÷ 12 = 1 remainder 6 → gcd(12, 6)
12 ÷ 6 = 2 remainder 0 → gcd(6, 0) = 6

Connection to L3: This is the fundamental operation used in GCD properties.

Connection to L5: This builds on division and remainder concepts.

Common mistakes: Not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly, using subtraction method (slower).

Real code pattern:
def gcd(a, b):
    a, b = abs(a), abs(b)
    while b:
        a, b = b, a % b
    return a

Python has built-in math.gcd() which uses similar algorithm.

examples.trace:
Step 1: 56 and 98, 98 > 56 so swap mentally or in algorithm
Actually Euclidean algorithm: gcd(56,98)
98 ÷ 56 = 1 remainder 42 → gcd(56,42)
56 ÷ 42 = 1 remainder 14 → gcd(42,14)
42 ÷ 14 = 3 remainder 0 → gcd(14,0) = 14
Check: 56÷14=4, 98÷14=7, both integer ✓
Alternative factorization: 56=2³×7, 98=2×7², common: 2×7=14

Step 1: gcd(81,153)
153 ÷ 81 = 1 remainder 72 → gcd(81,72)
81 ÷ 72 = 1 remainder 9 → gcd(72,9)
72 ÷ 9 = 8 remainder 0 → gcd(9,0) = 9
Check: 81÷9=9, 153÷9=17 ✓
Factorization: 81=3⁴, 153=3²×17, common: 3²=9

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is GCD and Subarray?
ladders.desc: Basic definitions of greatest common divisor and contiguous subarrays.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level introduces the two core concepts needed for the problem: GCD and subarrays.

GCD (Greatest Common Divisor): For two or more integers, the GCD is the largest positive integer that divides all of them without leaving a remainder. For example, GCD of 12 and 18 is 6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does.

Key points:
- Every number is divisible by 1, so GCD is always at least 1.
- If GCD is 1, numbers are called coprime or relatively prime (they share no common factors other than 1).
- GCD of a single number is the number itself (or its absolute value).
- GCD of 0 and a is |a| (by convention).

Subarray: A contiguous sequence of elements from an array. For array [a₀, a₁, a₂, ..., aₙ₋₁]:
- A subarray is defined by start index i and end index j where 0 ≤ i ≤ j < n.
- It includes all elements from i to j inclusive: [aᵢ, aᵢ₊₁, ..., aⱼ].
- Number of subarrays in array of length n is n(n+1)/2.

Examples:
Array [2, 4, 6] has subarrays:
Length 1: [2], [4], [6]
Length 2: [2,4], [4,6]
Length 3: [2,4,6]
Total: 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 = 3×4/2

For the problem, we need to find all subarrays where GCD of all elements equals 1.

Connection to L4: GCD computation builds on this definition.

Connection to higher levels: Understanding subarrays is necessary for counting them, and GCD=1 condition is what we're checking.

Common mistakes: Confusing subarray with subsequence (which doesn't require contiguity), forgetting that GCD of single element is the element itself, not understanding that GCD(0,x)=x.

Real code pattern: Basic understanding, no complex code yet.
# Subarray iteration pattern (for intuition):
for i in range(n):
    for j in range(i, n):
        subarray = arr[i:j+1]
        # check GCD of subarray

examples.input: Array [3, 6, 9], find subarrays and their GCDs
examples.trace:
Array length n=3, total subarrays = 3×4/2 = 6.
List all:
1. [3] (indices 0-0): GCD=3
2. [6] (1-1): GCD=6
3. [9] (2-2): GCD=9
4. [3,6] (0-1): GCD(3,6)=3
5. [6,9] (1-2): GCD(6,9)=3
6. [3,6,9] (0-2): GCD(3,6,9)=GCD(3,GCD(6,9))=GCD(3,3)=3
None have GCD=1, so answer would be 0 for 'count subarrays with GCD=1'.

Array [4,5]:
Subarrays:
[4]: GCD=4 ≠1
[5]: GCD=5 ≠1
[4,5]: GCD(4,5)=1 ✓
Only [4,5] has GCD=1, so count=1.
Total subarrays=2×3/2=3, only 1 meets condition.

testCases.input: Number of subarrays in array of length 4

approaches.name: Prefix GCD with HashMap O(n log M)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to store GCD values of all subarrays ending at previous positions. For each new element, compute GCD with all previous distinct GCD values and update counts. This avoids recomputation by reusing previous results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Subarrays with GCD = 1 Using Prefix GCD HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all subarrays where greatest common divisor equals 1 using hashmap to store GCD values of subarrays ending at previous positions.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all contiguous subarrays where the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all elements equals 1. The naive approach would check all O(n²) subarrays, resulting in O(n³) time with GCD computations. The optimized approach uses the observation that when extending a subarray by one element, the GCD can only decrease or stay the same, and there are at most O(log M) distinct GCD values for subarrays ending at any position (where M is the maximum array value). We maintain a hashmap that stores all possible GCD values for subarrays ending at the previous position, along with their frequencies. For each new element, we compute GCD with all previous distinct GCD values, creating a new hashmap for the current position. This reuses previous computations efficiently. The intuition: GCD is associative and monotonic when extending subarrays - gcd(gcd(a,b),c) = gcd(a,b,c). By tracking all GCDs ending at i-1, we can compute all GCDs ending at i in O(log M) time per element. The total count accumulates whenever we encounter GCD = 1 in any hashmap. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize total_count = 0 and prev_gcd_map = empty hashmap. 2. For each element arr[i] from left to right: a. Create curr_gcd_map starting with {arr[i]: 1}. b. For each (gcd_val, freq) in prev_gcd_map: compute new_gcd = gcd(gcd_val, arr[i]), add to curr_gcd_map[new_gcd] += freq. c. Add curr_gcd_map[1] to total_count if present. d. Set prev_gcd_map = curr_gcd_map. 3. Return total_count. Time complexity: O(n log M) where n is array length and M is maximum value - each element processes O(log M) distinct GCDs. Space: O(n log M) worst-case but typically O(log M) for the hashmaps. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into the complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling empty arrays, forgetting to include the single-element subarray, incorrectly merging GCD frequencies, or using O(n²) approach. Real pattern: Use defaultdict(int) for frequency maps, iterate through items() to avoid modification during iteration.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize total = 0, prev_map = {}. i=0 (element=2): curr_map starts {2:1}. prev_map empty. curr_map[1] doesn't exist. total remains 0. prev_map = {2:1}. i=1 (element=4): curr_map starts {4:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(2,4)=2, curr_map[2] += 1 → {4:1, 2:1}. curr_map[1] doesn't exist. total=0. prev_map = {4:1, 2:1}. i=2 (element=6): curr_map starts {6:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(4,6)=2 → curr_map[2] += 1 → {6:1, 2:1}. gcd(2,6)=2 → curr_map[2] += 1 → {6:1, 2:2}. curr_map[1] doesn't exist. total=0. Return 0.

Step 1: total=0, prev_map={}. i=0 (3): curr_map={3:1}. No 1. total=0. prev_map={3:1}. i=1 (6): curr_map starts {6:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(3,6)=3 → curr_map[3]+=1 → {6:1, 3:1}. No 1. total=0. prev_map={6:1, 3:1}. i=2 (2): curr_map starts {2:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(6,2)=2 → curr_map[2]+=1 → {2:2}. gcd(3,2)=1 → curr_map[1]+=1 → {2:2, 1:1}. curr_map[1]=1 → total+=1=1. prev_map={2:2, 1:1}. Now count subarrays: [2] itself has gcd=1 (from curr_map at i=2), plus subarrays ending at i=2 that include previous elements: [6,2] has gcd=2, [3,6,2] has gcd=1 (from gcd(3,2)=1). Wait, need to recount: Actually at i=2, curr_map after processing: {2:2, 1:1} means: subarray [2] has gcd=2 (single element), [6,2] has gcd=2, [3,6,2] has gcd=1. So only [3,6,2] contributes 1. But we also have earlier: at i=1, no gcd=1. At i=0, no gcd=1. So total should be 1? Let me recompute carefully. Initialize total=0. i=0: arr[0]=3. curr_map={3:1}. No gcd=1. total=0. prev_map={3:1}. i=1: arr[1]=6. curr_map starts {6:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(3,6)=3 → curr_map[3]=1. curr_map={6:1, 3:1}. No gcd=1. total=0. prev_map={6:1, 3:1}. i=2: arr[2]=2. curr_map starts {2:1}. Process prev_map items: (6,1): gcd(6,2)=2 → curr_map[2]+=1=2. (3,1): gcd(3,2)=1 → curr_map[1]=1. curr_map={2:2, 1:1}. curr_map[1]=1 → total+=1=1. Return 1. But expected output is 3. Let me list all subarrays: [3] gcd=3, [6] gcd=6, [2] gcd=2, [3,6] gcd=3, [6,2] gcd=2, [3,6,2] gcd=1. Only one subarray has gcd=1. So output should be 1. The problem statement might have different interpretation? Wait, the problem is 'Find number of subarrays with gcd = 1'. For [3,6,2], subarrays: (3), (6), (2), (3,6), (6,2), (3,6,2). Only (3,6,2) has gcd=1. So answer is 1. But the example says 3. Let me check: Actually, I made mistake. The algorithm counts ALL subarrays ending at each position that have gcd=1. At i=2, we have curr_map[1]=1 from processing (3,1) meaning the subarray [3,6,2] has gcd=1. But we also have subarrays ending at earlier positions? Let's trace properly: We need to accumulate total at EACH position. At i=0: curr_map={3:1}, no 1. total=0. At i=1: curr_map={6:1, 3:1}, no 1. total=0. At i=2: curr_map={2:1} initially, then after processing: from (6,1): gcd=2 → {2:2}, from (3,1): gcd=1 → {2:2, 1:1}. So curr_map[1]=1. total+=1=1. So total=1. But expected is 3. Something wrong. Let me reconsider the algorithm: Actually, we should include the single element [2]? gcd(2)=2≠1. [6,2] gcd=2≠1. Only [3,6,2] gcd=1. So answer 1 seems correct. Perhaps the example input [3,6,2] should output 1, not 3. I'll correct: For [3,6,2], output is 1. Let me use a different example that yields 3. Use [2,3,6]: Subarrays: [2] gcd=2, [3] gcd=3, [6] gcd=6, [2,3] gcd=1, [3,6] gcd=3, [2,3,6] gcd=1. That's 2 subarrays with gcd=1, not 3. To get 3: [1,2,3] has subarrays: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3] all gcd=1 → 3. So I'll change example to [1,2,3]. Step-by-step for [1,2,3]: Initialize total=0, prev_map={}. i=0 (1): curr_map={1:1}. curr_map[1]=1 → total+=1=1. prev_map={1:1}. i=1 (2): curr_map starts {2:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(1,2)=1 → curr_map[1]+=1 → {2:1, 1:1}. curr_map[1]=1 → total+=1=2. prev_map={2:1, 1:1}. i=2 (3): curr_map starts {3:1}. Process prev_map: gcd(2,3)=1 → curr_map[1]+=1 → {3:1, 1:1}. gcd(1,3)=1 → curr_map[1]+=1 → {3:1, 1:2}. curr_map[1]=2 → total+=2=4. Wait, that gives total=4, but expected is 3. Let's list: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3] are 3. Why total=4? Because at i=2, curr_map[1]=2 represents two subarrays ending at index 2 with gcd=1: [2,3]? gcd(2,3)=1 and [1,2,3] gcd=1. But [2,3] is not a subarray ending at index 2? Actually [2,3] ends at index 2. So subarrays with gcd=1: [1], [1,2], [2,3], [1,2,3] = 4. Yes, [2,3] has gcd=1. So total=4. So [1,2,3] yields 4. To get exactly 3, use [1,2,4]: Subarrays: [1], [1,2], [1,2,4] have gcd=1 → 3. Trace [1,2,4]: i=0: curr_map={1:1}, total=1. prev_map={1:1}. i=1: curr_map starts {2:1}, process: gcd(1,2)=1 → curr_map[1]=1 → {2:1, 1:1}. total+=1=2. prev_map={2:1, 1:1}. i=2: curr_map starts {4:1}, process: gcd(2,4)=2 → curr_map[2]=1 → {4:1, 2:1}. gcd(1,4)=1 → curr_map[1]=1 → {4:1, 2:1, 1:1}. total+=1=3. So output 3. I'll use [1,2,4] as example with output 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintain GCD Frequency Map for Subarrays Ending at Each Position
ladders.desc: Compute and update hashmap of GCD values for all subarrays ending at current index using previous map.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core computational step in the full solution. Given the previous hashmap (prev_map) that stores all distinct GCD values for subarrays ending at index i-1 and their frequencies, and the current element arr[i], we need to compute a new hashmap (curr_map) for subarrays ending at index i. The intuition: Any subarray ending at i can be formed by taking a subarray ending at i-1 and appending arr[i], or by starting a new subarray at i. Therefore, curr_map must include: 1. The single-element subarray [arr[i]] with GCD = arr[i]. 2. For each GCD value g in prev_map with frequency f, the GCD of extending those subarrays by arr[i] is gcd(g, arr[i]). Since multiple previous subarrays might produce the same new GCD, we accumulate frequencies. This leverages the associative property of GCD: gcd(g, arr[i]) for each previous subarray. The key insight is that the number of distinct GCD values in prev_map is bounded by O(log M), making this efficient. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize curr_map as empty dictionary or defaultdict(int). 2. Add curr_map[arr[i]] = 1 for the single-element subarray. 3. For each key-value pair (g, f) in prev_map: a. Compute new_gcd = gcd(g, arr[i]). b. Increment curr_map[new_gcd] by f. 4. Return curr_map. Time complexity: O(k) where k is number of distinct GCDs in prev_map, which is O(log M). Space: O(log M) for curr_map. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each array element in the main loop. Common mistakes: Forgetting to include the single-element subarray, modifying prev_map while iterating (should iterate over items()), or not using efficient GCD computation. Real pattern: Use math.gcd in Python, iterate as 'for g, f in prev_map.items():'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize curr_map = {}. Step 2: Add single-element: curr_map[2] = 1 → {2:1}. Step 3: Iterate prev_map items: First (6,1): new_gcd = gcd(6,2) = 2. curr_map[2] += 1 → {2:2}. Second (3,1): new_gcd = gcd(3,2) = 1. curr_map[1] = 1 → {2:2, 1:1}. Return {2:2, 1:1}.
Step 1: curr_map = {}. Step 2: curr_map[4] = 1 → {4:1}. Step 3: Iterate prev_map: (1,2): new_gcd = gcd(1,4) = 1. curr_map[1] += 2 → {4:1, 1:2}. Return {4:1, 1:2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Distinct GCD Values are Limited (O(log M)) for Subarrays Ending at a Position
ladders.desc: Understand why number of distinct GCD values for subarrays ending at any index is bounded by O(log M), enabling efficient hashmap approach.
ladders.explanation: This core insight makes the O(n log M) algorithm possible. For any fixed position i, consider all subarrays ending at i (i.e., subarrays arr[j...i] for j ≤ i). Their GCD values form a sequence as j decreases from i to 0. Let g_j = gcd(arr[j], arr[j+1], ..., arr[i]). As j decreases (extending subarray leftward), the GCD can only decrease or stay the same because gcd(a,b) ≤ min(a,b). More importantly, each time the GCD changes, it decreases by at least a factor of 2 (for integer GCDs). Proof sketch: Suppose g_j and g_{j-1} are consecutive distinct GCDs. Then g_{j-1} = gcd(arr[j-1], g_j). Since g_j divides all elements from j to i, and arr[j-1] might not be divisible by g_j, the new GCD g_{j-1} must be a proper divisor of g_j. For integers, any proper divisor is at most half the original value (except for 1). Thus, the GCD can change at most O(log M) times where M is the maximum array value. This bound ensures that the hashmap storing GCD frequencies has size O(log M), not O(n). In practice, the number is even smaller. This property allows us to maintain only O(log M) entries per position, making the overall algorithm O(n log M) instead of O(n²). Connection to L1: This bound justifies why processing prev_map of size O(log M) is efficient. Common mistakes: Assuming unlimited distinct GCDs, or not understanding the halving property. Real pattern: When implementing, we rely on this bound for complexity analysis.

examples.input: Array ending at index with values: [12, 8, 4] (subarrays: [4], [8,4], [12,8,4])
examples.trace: Compute GCDs for subarrays ending at last element (4): Single [4]: gcd=4. [8,4]: gcd(8,4)=4. [12,8,4]: gcd(12,4)=4. All GCDs are 4. Distinct count=1, which is ≤ log2(12)≈3.6.

examples.input: Array ending at index with values: [6, 4, 2]
examples.trace: Subarrays ending at last element (2): [2]: gcd=2. [4,2]: gcd(4,2)=2. [6,4,2]: gcd(6,2)=2. All 2. Distinct=1 ≤ log2(6)≈2.6.

testCases.expected: log2(16)=4, so at most 4 distinct GCDs
log2(100)≈6.64, so at most 7 distinct GCDs
4 distinct, log2(12)≈3.6, so 4 is within bound
4 distinct, log2(8)=3, so 4 is within bound (tight)

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute GCD of Two Numbers Efficiently
ladders.desc: Implement Euclidean algorithm to compute greatest common divisor of two integers.
ladders.explanation: GCD (Greatest Common Divisor) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both a and b without remainder. The Euclidean algorithm is based on the principle that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). This reduces the problem recursively until b becomes 0, at which point gcd(a,0)=a. The intuition: If d divides both a and b, then it also divides a-b, and more generally a mod b. By repeatedly replacing (a,b) with (b, a mod b), we reduce the numbers quickly. The algorithm runs in O(log min(a,b)) time because the numbers decrease exponentially. Step-by-step: 1. While b ≠ 0: a, b = b, a % b. 2. Return a (which is the GCD). Alternatively, use recursion: gcd(a,b) = a if b==0 else gcd(b, a%b). For non-negative integers, ensure a ≥ b initially, but the algorithm works regardless as first step swaps them if needed. Time complexity: O(log min(a,b)) due to exponential reduction. Space: O(1) iterative, O(log min(a,b)) recursive. Connection to L2: GCD computation is used repeatedly in the hashmap update. Common mistakes: Not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating variables correctly, or using slow naive method. Real pattern: In Python, use math.gcd() built-in, but understanding the algorithm is key.

examples.trace: Step 1: b=98 ≠0. Compute a,b = 98, 56%98=56. Now a=98, b=56. Step 2: b=56≠0. Compute a,b = 56, 98%56=42. Now a=56, b=42. Step 3: b=42≠0. Compute a,b = 42, 56%42=14. Now a=42, b=14. Step 4: b=14≠0. Compute a,b = 14, 42%14=0. Now a=14, b=0. Step 5: b=0, return a=14.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through Dictionary Items Without Modification
ladders.desc: Safely access key-value pairs from a hashmap/dictionary while potentially updating the same or another dictionary.
ladders.explanation: In many algorithms, including our GCD subarray problem, we need to iterate over all entries in a dictionary (hashmap) and use them to compute new values for a different dictionary. A common mistake is modifying the dictionary while iterating over it, which can cause runtime errors or incorrect behavior. The safe pattern is to iterate over a snapshot of the items, typically using .items() method which returns a view, but since we're not modifying the iterated dictionary, it's safe. However, if we were modifying the same dictionary, we'd need to copy items first. In our case, we iterate over prev_map to compute curr_map, so prev_map remains unchanged during iteration. The intuition: Dictionaries are mutable, and iterators can become invalid if the dictionary changes size during iteration. By iterating over .items(), we get each key-value pair as a tuple, and we can freely modify other dictionaries. Step-by-step: 1. Obtain items view: pairs = prev_map.items(). 2. Loop: for key, value in pairs: # use key and value to compute new_gcd. 3. Update curr_map[new_gcd] += value. Time complexity: O(k) where k is dictionary size. Space: O(1) extra for iteration. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the GCD update routine. Common mistakes: Modifying prev_map during iteration, using .keys() and then looking up values inefficiently, or not handling missing keys in curr_map (use defaultdict or .get()). Real pattern: In Python, 'for g, f in prev_map.items():' is idiomatic.

examples.input: prev_map = {2:3, 4:1}, compute new_gcd = gcd(g,5) for each, add to curr_map
examples.trace: Initialize curr_map = {}. Iterate items: First (2,3): new_gcd = gcd(2,5)=1. curr_map[1] = 3. Second (4,1): new_gcd = gcd(4,5)=1. curr_map[1] += 1 = 4. Also add single-element: curr_map[5]=1. So curr_map = {5:1, 1:4}.

examples.input: prev_map = {6:2}, compute new_gcd = gcd(g,3), add to curr_map
examples.trace: Initialize curr_map = {}. Add single: curr_map[3]=1. Iterate: (6,2): new_gcd = gcd(6,3)=3. curr_map[3] += 2 = 3. So curr_map = {3:3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subarray?
ladders.desc: Define subarray as a contiguous subsequence of an array.
ladders.explanation: A subarray is a contiguous part of an array. Given an array arr of length n, a subarray is defined by two indices i and j (0 ≤ i ≤ j < n) and consists of elements arr[i], arr[i+1], ..., arr[j]. It must be contiguous, meaning the elements are consecutive in the original array. This is different from a subsequence, which can have elements non-contiguous. For example, in array [1,2,3,4], some subarrays are [1], [2,3], [1,2,3,4]. But [1,3] is not a subarray because it skips element 2. The number of subarrays in an array of length n is n*(n+1)/2, because for each starting index i (0 to n-1) and ending index j (i to n-1), there is exactly one subarray. Counting subarrays is a common problem pattern. Intuition: Think of sliding windows or all possible intervals. Connection to L4: In our problem, we count subarrays with a certain property (GCD=1). Common mistakes: Confusing subarray with subsequence, or including non-contiguous elements. Real pattern: In code, subarrays are often generated by nested loops: for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): process arr[i:j+1].

examples.trace: Length n=3. Number of subarrays = 3*4/2=6. List by starting index: i=0: j=0 → [5], j=1 → [5,6], j=2 → [5,6,7]. i=1: j=1 → [6], j=2 → [6,7]. i=2: j=2 → [7]. Total 6.
Length n=1. Number = 1*2/2=1. Only subarray is the whole array: [10].
